stem: Why can rapidly flowing blood be dark on a spin-echo image?

options.text: Blood contains no hydrogen to excite
Flowing spins always have a T1 of zero
Excited spins may leave the slice before the refocusing pulse or echo is received
The receiver rejects all frequencies produced by motion

explanation: Spin echo requires spins to experience the excitation, refocusing pulse, and readout within the selected slice. Fast flow can carry them away before refocusing, producing a flow void.

objective: Relate flow void to slice geometry.
stem: Which change makes through-plane high-velocity signal loss less likely?

options.text: Use a thinner slice and lengthen the time to the refocusing pulse
Increase the number of phase steps only
Lengthen the interval before the refocusing pulse
Use a thicker slice or shorter time to the refocusing pulse

explanation: A thicker slice gives moving blood farther to travel before leaving, and earlier refocusing gives it less time to escape. Thin slices and long delays make loss of the required 180-degree pulse more likely.

stem: Why can turbulent or complex flow cause signal loss even when blood remains inside the slice?

options.text: Different velocities and directions create a broad distribution of phases within the voxel
All spins acquire exactly the same phase
Turbulence eliminates the applied gradients
The tissue's proton density becomes zero

explanation: A voxel containing many velocities accumulates inconsistent phase shifts during gradients. Their vector signals cancel, causing intravoxel dephasing and dark signal.

stem: Which factors most directly influence whether flowing spins remain available for a spin echo?

options.text: Display window, monitor brightness, and image annotation
Flow velocity, slice thickness, and echo timing
Only patient age and magnet manufacturer
Phase matrix alone, independent of motion direction

explanation: Signal depends on whether spins traverse the excited slice before refocusing and readout. Their speed, travel distance through the slice, and elapsed time therefore matter.

stem: Why can gradient-echo imaging show inflowing blood as bright?

options.text: Flowing blood is permanently phase locked to stationary tissue
Gradient echo suppresses all inflow effects
Fresh spins enter with relatively unsaturated longitudinal magnetization and no 180-degree pulse is required
Blood has a higher gyromagnetic ratio than tissue hydrogen

explanation: Repeated short-TR excitation saturates stationary tissue, while fresh blood entering the slice can produce stronger signal. Gradient echo does not require the same spin to remain for a later 180-degree pulse.
